name: Python
scope: An INTERMEDIATE Python developer, with 2-5 years experience, has a solid grasp of core programming concepts like data types, control flow, functions (including lambdas), and is adept with data structures such as lists, dictionaries, and sets. They are comfortable with object-oriented programming, able to create classes, implement inheritance, and understand polymorphism. They can manage modules and packages, use Python's standard library effectively, and handle third-party packages with tools like pip and virtual environments. Proficient in file operations and data serialization (JSON, CSV), they can interact with databases using SQL and ORMs like SQLAlchemy. They develop simple web applications using frameworks like Flask, consume and create APIs, and understand basic networking and concurrency (threading, multiprocessing). They write unit tests using unittest or pytest, debug code efficiently, and adhere to code quality standards like PEP 8 and best practices in documentation. Familiar with version control systems like Git, they collaborate using platforms like GitHub, participate in code reviews, and communicate effectively within a team. They can automate tasks with scripts, optimize code for performance, contribute to system design discussions, and improve existing systems while maintaining security awareness. Continuously learning, they stay updated with Python developments, seek feedback, and are capable of independent problem-solving on moderate-complexity projects.
long_scope:
An individual at the INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in Python is expected to have moved beyond the basics and can apply Python skills to solve real-world problems. They have a solid understanding of core concepts and can contribute meaningfully to projects. Below is a comprehensive scope of what an intermediate Python developer should be able to accomplish:

⸻

Core Programming Skills
	•	Data Types and Structures
	•	Proficient use of fundamental data types: integers, floats, strings, booleans.
	•	Advanced manipulation of data structures: lists, tuples, dictionaries, sets.
	•	Understanding of immutable vs mutable types.
	•	Control Flow
	•	Mastery of loops (for, while) and control statements (if, elif, else).
	•	Utilization of list comprehensions and generator expressions for efficient code.
	•	Functions
	•	Defining and invoking functions with positional, keyword, and default arguments.
	•	Understanding variable scope and lifetime.
	•	Use of anonymous functions (lambda).
	•	Modules and Packages
	•	Importing and organizing code using modules and packages.
	•	Familiarity with Python’s standard library modules (e.g., os, sys, datetime).
	•	Error and Exception Handling
	•	Using try, except, finally, and else blocks to handle exceptions.
	•	Raising exceptions and creating custom exception classes.

⸻

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
	•	Classes and Objects
	•	Creating classes with attributes and methods.
	•	Instantiating objects and accessing class members.
	•	Inheritance and Polymorphism
	•	Implementing single and multiple inheritance.
	•	Overriding methods and using super() to extend functionality.
	•	Understanding polymorphism and its application in Python.
	•	Encapsulation
	•	Using access modifiers to control access to class members.
	•	Understanding of getters and setters.

⸻

File and Data Handling
	•	File Operations
	•	Reading from and writing to text and binary files.
	•	Using context managers (with statement) for file operations.
	•	Data Serialization
	•	Working with JSON, CSV, and XML data formats.
	•	Serializing and deserializing data using json and pickle modules.
	•	Data Manipulation
	•	Using libraries like pandas and NumPy for data analysis.
	•	Performing data cleaning and transformation tasks.

⸻

Third-Party Libraries and Tools
	•	Package Management
	•	Installing and managing packages using pip.
	•	Understanding virtual environments (venv, virtualenv, conda) to manage dependencies.
	•	Popular Libraries
	•	Utilizing requests for HTTP operations.
	•	Implementing regular expressions with the re module.
	•	Basic plotting with matplotlib.

⸻

Web Development Basics
	•	Frameworks
	•	Building simple web applications using Flask.
	•	Understanding the basics of routing, templating, and handling HTTP requests.
	•	APIs
	•	Consuming RESTful APIs.
	•	Creating simple APIs to expose data or services.

⸻

Database Interaction
	•	SQL and NoSQL Databases
	•	Connecting to databases like SQLite or PostgreSQL.
	•	Performing CRUD operations using SQL queries.
	•	ORMs
	•	Using Object-Relational Mapping tools like SQLAlchemy to interact with databases.

⸻

Testing and Debugging
	•	Unit Testing
	•	Writing tests using unittest or pytest frameworks.
	•	Understanding test cases, test suites, and test runners.
	•	Debugging Techniques
	•	Using debugging tools or IDE features to step through code.
	•	Interpreting stack traces and logging output.

⸻

Version Control and Collaboration
	•	Git Proficiency
	•	Cloning repositories, committing changes, pushing, and pulling updates.
	•	Managing branches and understanding merge conflicts.
	•	Collaboration Tools
	•	Participating in code reviews using platforms like GitHub or GitLab.
	•	Documenting code changes and writing clear commit messages.

⸻

Code Quality and Best Practices
	•	PEP 8 Compliance
	•	Writing code that adheres to Python’s style guidelines for readability.
	•	Documentation
	•	Writing meaningful docstrings and inline comments.
	•	Generating documentation using tools like Sphinx.
	•	Refactoring
	•	Improving code structure and efficiency without altering external behavior.

⸻

Problem-Solving Skills
	•	Algorithmic Thinking
	•	Applying common algorithms and data structures to solve problems.
	•	Optimizing code for performance and scalability.
	•	Project Contributions
	•	Breaking down tasks into manageable components.
	•	Estimating time and resources required for development tasks.

⸻

Networking and Concurrency
	•	Basic Networking
	•	Understanding sockets and basic network communication.
	•	Working with higher-level network protocols.
	•	Concurrency
	•	Implementing threading and multiprocessing for concurrent execution.
	•	Understanding the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) and its implications.

⸻

Automation and Scripting
	•	Task Automation
	•	Writing scripts to automate routine tasks (file management, data parsing).
	•	System Interaction
	•	Using Python to interact with the operating system and execute shell commands.

⸻

Security Awareness
	•	Secure Coding Practices
	•	Understanding common security vulnerabilities (e.g., injection attacks).
	•	Handling sensitive data securely (e.g., using environment variables).

⸻

Soft Skills and Teamwork
	•	Communication
	•	Effectively communicating technical concepts to team members.
	•	Continuous Learning
	•	Keeping up-to-date with the latest Python developments and libraries.
	•	Seeking feedback and actively improving coding skills.

⸻

Expected Tasks and Outcomes
	•	Develop Moderate-Complexity Applications
	•	Building applications that integrate multiple components (e.g., data processing pipeline).
	•	Maintain and Improve Existing Systems
	•	Debugging and fixing issues in existing codebases.
	•	Enhancing features and optimizing performance.
	•	Contribute to Design Discussions
	•	Participating in planning and architectural meetings.
	•	Providing input on technology choices and implementation strategies.

⸻

Tools and Technologies
	•	IDEs and Editors
	•	Proficient with tools like PyCharm, Visual Studio Code, or Sublime Text.
	•	Version Control Systems
	•	Comfortable using Git and platforms like GitHub.
	•	Databases
	•	Experience with relational databases (SQLite, PostgreSQL).
	•	Testing Frameworks
	•	Familiarity with unittest and pytest.
	•	Containers and Virtualization
	•	Basic understanding of Docker for containerization.

⸻

Mastery Over Concepts
	•	Efficient Coding
	•	Writing optimized and efficient code using appropriate data structures and algorithms.
	•	Modular Design
	•	Organizing code into reusable modules and packages.
	•	Error Handling
	•	Anticipating potential errors and writing robust code to handle exceptions gracefully.

⸻

Technical Expertise
	•	Independent Problem Solving
	•	Tackling assignments without constant supervision.
	•	Debugging Proficiency
	•	Quickly identifying and resolving code issues.
	•	Best Practices Application
	•	Implementing industry-standard practices in coding and development workflows.

⸻

Designing Systems and Processes
	•	System Design
	•	Contributing to the design of applications and systems.
	•	Process Improvement
	•	Suggesting and implementing improvements to development processes and workflows.

⸻

An intermediate Python developer is a valuable team member capable of handling a variety of programming tasks. They are not only able to write functional code but also contribute to the overall development process through effective communication, adherence to best practices, and a commitment to continuous learning.

name: Vector Databases
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in Vector Databases as a competency should be able to design, build, operate and troubleshoot an end-to-end vector-search stack for a product with minimal supervision. They grasp vector-space maths, curse of dimensionality, metric vs non-metric distances, and can benchmark cosine, inner-product, Euclidean, Jaccard or Hamming similarity with Python, NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn and FAISS. They create, fine-tune and version embeddings from OpenAI, Hugging Face, Cohere or Sentence-Transformers, run ONNX Runtime batches, track experiments in MLFlow/Weights & Biases, and inspect quality with t-SNE/UMAP. They model hybrid schemas, chunk, deduplicate and ETL data via dbt, Airflow, Kafka Connect or Spark into Pinecone, Weaviate, Milvus, Qdrant, Elasticsearch or pgvector while attaching rich metadata for BM25/keyword filters. They select and tune HNSW (M, ef), IVF-PQ/IVF-Flat (nlist, nprobe), Annoy, ScaNN, DiskANN or brute-force indexes, apply product/scalar quantisation, estimate memory and run recall-latency experiments. They implement retrieval APIs in FastAPI, Flask, Spring or Express, support k-NN, range and multi-vector queries, cache with Redis or Cloudflare, and re-rank via cross-encoders, LLMs or MMR. They wire Retrieval-Augmented Generation with LangChain or LlamaIndex, craft prompts, adjust chunk overlap/top-k, and measure BLEU, ROUGE or human feedback to curb hallucinations. They provision multi-node clusters using Kubernetes operators/Helm and Terraform on AWS/GCP/Azure, manage shards, replication, rolling upgrades, autoscaling and load-test with locust or k6. They observe systems through OpenTelemetry, Prometheus, Grafana or Datadog, set SLOs, sample golden queries, debug with Sentry, Honeycomb or pdb/dlv and handle index corruption, memory pressure or embedding drift. They secure pipelines with TLS, KMS, IAM/RBAC, k8s network policies, OPA, Vault, redact PII, apply differential privacy and optimise TCO by right-sizing compute, cold-tier storage, deduplication and compression. They automate CI/CD via GitHub Actions, GitLab, Jenkins or Argo CD, version data with DVC/lakeFS, exchange vectors in Arrow, Parquet, HDF5 or FAISS IO, write clear design docs/RFCs, map business KPIs to recall@k and mentor juniors.
long_scope:
COMPETENCY SCOPE  
VECTOR DATABASES – INTERMEDIATE (≈ 3-5 years of hands-on experience)

An INTERMEDIATE practitioner can be trusted to design, build, operate, troubleshoot and optimize a complete production-grade vector-search stack for a single product line or use-case, working largely without supervision. They exhibit solid command of the fundamentals and can apply them pragmatically, but have not yet acquired the deep algorithmic, cross-vendor or research-oriented breadth expected of ADVANCED and EXPERT levels.

The scope below is organized into 14 capability domains.  
For every domain the scope expresses:  
• What the practitioner should be able to accomplish (outcomes)  
• The kinds of tasks they should perform end-to-end (skills)  
• The concepts they must have mastered to do so (knowledge)  
• Representative tools/technologies they are expected to wield confidently  
(Names in brackets are illustrative of what they may already have used, not an exhaustive or prescriptive list.)

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
1. Core Vector Concepts & Mathematical Foundations
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Confidently reason about how vector spaces, distance metrics and similarity search work and articulate trade-offs to non-experts.  
• Detect and avoid incorrect distance/metric choices that would silently undermine retrieval quality.

Tasks / Skills
• Choose appropriate distance metrics (cosine, inner-product, Euclidean, Manhattan, Jaccard, Hamming).  
• Normalise / scale vectors, handle dimensionality differences, apply L2 normalisation or unit vectors when required.  
• Compute recall / precision curves for various metrics and k-values.

Knowledge
• Curse of dimensionality; triangle inequality; metric vs. non-metric distances; locality-sensitive hashing intuition; vector quantisation intuition, including residuals and product quantisation.

Tools / Tech
• Python / NumPy / SciPy, FAISS, scikit-learn, Matlab/R for quick experimentation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
2. Embedding Generation & Management
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Produce fit-for-purpose embeddings from text, images, audio or multimodal sources and manage their lifecycle.

Tasks / Skills
• Select or fine-tune embedding models (e.g., OpenAI, Hugging Face, Cohere, Sentence-Transformers).  
• Implement deterministic pipelines that tokenize, batch, embed and post-process features (mean-pooling, max-pooling, CLS token handling).  
• Track embedding model versions; trigger re-indexing when models upgrade.  
• Evaluate embedding quality via clustering, t-SNE/UMAP visualisation and downstream task metrics.

Knowledge
• Token vs. sub-token embeddings; pooled vs. per-token vectors; semantic drift on re-training; domain adaptation; model size vs. performance trade-offs; throughput vs. latency in embedding generation.

Tools / Tech
• Hugging Face Transformers, SentencePiece/BPE tokenisers, ONNX Runtime, GPU/CPU batchers, MLFlow / Weights & Biases for experiment tracking.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
3. Data Modelling, Schemas & Metadata
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Design vector-augmented schemas that combine dense embeddings with rich structured metadata for hybrid filtering.

Tasks / Skills
• Model metadata attributes and secondary indexes; configure payload schemas in vendors like Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus, Elasticsearch or pgvector.  
• Develop ingestion jobs that validate, deduplicate, chunk (for text), window (audio), and attach metadata.  
• Derive chunk sizes via empirical evaluation of semantic coverage vs. token limits.

Knowledge
• Hybrid search theory (vector + keyword/BM25); late vs. early fusion; cardinality implications; compound filtering logic; primary key vs. vector key semantics.

Tools / Tech
• dbt, Airflow, Kafka Connect, Spark/PySpark ETL, SQL/DSL of chosen vector store.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
4. Index Selection & Configuration
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Create and tune indexes that meet specified recall, latency, memory and cost requirements at production scale.

Tasks / Skills
• Select index families (HNSW, IVF-PQ/IVF-Flat, Annoy, ScaNN, DiskANN, Hierarchical Navigable Small World, Flat brute-force).  
• Configure key hyper-parameters: M, efConstruction/efSearch (HNSW); nlist, nprobe (IVF); num_leaves, search_k (ScaNN).  
• Execute empirical benchmark loops and plot latency-recall trade-offs.

Knowledge
• Insert-time vs. query-time parameter impacts; memory footprint estimation; read-/write-amplification; re-index vs. incremental update strategies.

Tools / Tech
• FAISS benchmarking suite, Weaviate HNSW dashboards, Qdrant optimizer, Pinecone index metrics, ELK + Grafana for instrumentation.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
5. Query Processing & Retrieval Workflows
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Build robust retrieval pipelines (REST, gRPC, GraphQL or embedded library) that power downstream applications such as RAG, semantic search, recommendations or anomaly detection.

Tasks / Skills
• Compose queries with filter clauses, geo-spatial constraints, time weighting, freshness decay.  
• Implement query re-ranking (cross-encoders, LLM re-ranks) and late-stage fusion.  
• Build orchestration wrappers (FastAPI, Flask, Spring Boot, ExpressJS) and integrate auth, throttling and caching.

Knowledge
• k-NN vs. range queries; approximate vs. exact recall guarantees; multi-vector queries (average, max, polling).  
• Re-ranking models’ computational budgets; vector caching strategies (Champion-Challenger, write-through).

Tools / Tech
• Transformers re-rankers, Hugging Face Inference Endpoints, Redis-vector cache, Cloudflare CDN L3 cache.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
6. Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) Integration
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Deliver a working RAG system with controllable hallucination rates and measurable answer quality.

Tasks / Skills
• Construct retrieval chains (e.g., LangChain, LlamaIndex) combining vector store retrieval with prompt templating.  
• Experiment with chunk overlap, top-k vs. top-p retrieval, Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) for diversity.  
• Log and evaluate answer relevance through human feedback loops or automated metrics (BLEU, Rouge, factuality checks).

Knowledge
• Prompt engineering for source attribution; context-window budgeting; input/output token economics.  
• Failure cases: embedding drift, negative prompting, context collision.

Tools / Tech
• LangChain, LlamaIndex, OpenAI Function-calling, Cohere RAG toolkit, Promptlayer or Helicone for logging.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
7. Scalability, Distribution & High Availability
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Provision and operate multi-node clusters that maintain SLA under tens of millions to low-hundreds of millions of vectors.

Tasks / Skills
• Configure sharding/partitioning strategies (range, hash, hybrid) and replication factors.  
• Perform rolling upgrades with zero-/low-downtime; manage re-balancing and re-sharding events.  
• Conduct load tests (locust, k6) and capacity planning exercises.

Knowledge
• Consistency models of specific vendors; write path vs. read path; eventual vs. strong consistency; segment merging; vector splitting across disks.  
• Index rebuild vs. incremental catch-up costs; data locality vs. cross-zone latency.

Tools / Tech
• Kubernetes helm charts/operators for Milvus/Pinecone/Qdrant; Terraform IaC; AWS/GCP/Azure managed services; autoscaler policies.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
8. Performance Monitoring & Observability
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Maintain actionable dashboards and alerts on latency, QPS, recall, memory, CPU/GPU usage and index health.

Tasks / Skills
• Instrument code with OpenTelemetry; export vendor metrics; set SLOs and track error budgets.  
• Implement sampling for golden queries to detect silent degradation of recall or relevance.  
• Root-cause high-tail latency events.

Knowledge
• Index segmentation and compaction impact on latency; warm vs. cold cache behaviours; vector cardinality.

Tools / Tech
• Prometheus, Grafana, Datadog, Loki, Kibana, vendor SaaS dashboards.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
9. Security, Compliance & Governance
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Deploy vector stores and pipelines that satisfy organisational security posture and regulatory standards.

Tasks / Skills
• Implement encryption at rest and in transit (TLS, KMS keys).  
• Configure IAM roles, fine-grained RBAC, API keys, network policies (k8s, security groups).  
• Manage PII/PHI redaction prior to embedding; maintain audit trails for data lineage.

Knowledge
• Data residency requirements; impact of partial encryption on k-NN search; differential privacy on embeddings; model inversion risks.

Tools / Tech
• Hashicorp Vault, OPA/Gatekeeper, AWS IAM, Azure Managed Identity, GCP IAM, GDPR tooling.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
10. Cost Optimisation
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Control compute, storage and inference costs of embeddings and index operations.

Tasks / Skills
• Compare SaaS vs. self-host vs. serverless pricing models.  
• Right-size instance types, adjust replica counts, select cheaper storage tiers for cold segments.  
• Establish policies for vector deduplication, compression (PQ, scalar quantisation).

Knowledge
• TCO components: model inference, GPU utilisation, egress charges, snapshot storage; cost vs. recall trade-off.

Tools / Tech
• AWS Cost Explorer, GCP Cost Management, internal chargeback dashboards.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
11. DevOps & Automation
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Ship CI/CD pipelines that test, build, deploy and rollback vector search services safely.

Tasks / Skills
• Write infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi, CloudFormation).  
• Implement automated integration tests that embed sample corpora, query gold-set and assert recall.  
• Tag and version indexes, embeddings and models; automate blue-green or canary releases.

Knowledge
• Data vs. code versioning; immutability; reproducibility of vector snapshots; ACL propagation.

Tools / Tech
• GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, Argo CD, DVC, lakeFS.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
12. Interoperability & Data Movement
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Move vectors and metadata between environments, vendors or index formats without quality loss.

Tasks / Skills
• Export/import vectors in common formats (numpy arrays, HDF5, parquet, Arrow IPC).  
• Convert indexes via FAISS IO, HNSWlib, Annoy to vendor-specific back-ends.  
• Handle schema migration, version negotiation and backward compatibility.

Knowledge
• Quantisation incompatibilities, endianess, floating-point precision impact, 32-bit vs. 16-bit vectors.

Tools / Tech
• Apache Arrow, Parquet, HNSWlib, FAISS IO utilities.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
13. Troubleshooting & Incident Response
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Rapidly diagnose and remediate production incidents involving correctness, performance or availability.

Tasks / Skills
• Interpret stack traces, core dumps, or vendor error logs.  
• Reproduce faulty queries with deterministic seeds; bisect index snapshots; roll back faulty deployments.  
• Communicate RCA and preventive actions.

Knowledge
• Failure modes: index corruption, memory pressure, out-of-range vector values, embedding model drift, coordinate explosions.

Tools / Tech
• Sentry, Honeycomb, dlv/pdb debuggers, vendor diagnostic CLIs.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
14. Collaboration & Communication
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Outcomes
• Work cross-functionally with ML engineers, product managers, SREs and security teams to deliver vector-powered features.

Tasks / Skills
• Translate business relevance metrics (CTR, dwell time, CSAT) into retrieval KPIs (recall@10, MRR).  
• Write clear design docs, RFCs, post-mortems, runbooks.  
• Mentor juniors on vector basics; accept feedback; present benchmark results to stakeholders.

Knowledge
• Agile ceremonies; architecture review processes; decision records; stakeholder management.

Tools / Tech
• Confluence, Miro, draw.io, Google Docs/Slides, Slack, JIRA.

──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Summary of Proficiency Level
──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
An INTERMEDIATE practitioner:  
• Understands the WHY and the HOW of vector search well enough to own a full pipeline and production deployment.  
• Can articulate and defend design choices with data, design for moderate scale (10⁶–10⁸ vectors), and maintain SLAs.  
• Has encountered and solved real-world issues (index tuning, drift, cost overruns, security gaps) but has not yet authored novel algorithms, built multi-petabyte clusters, or published cutting-edge research, which sit in ADVANCED/EXPERT scopes.
